mnemonics: R-P-F: Rank, PageRank, Factors. Think of a 'Really Powerful Formula' that determines a page's importance.
TF-IDF: Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency. Remember 'Too Frequent? It's Definitely Found' for how common vs. unique terms are weighted.
HITS: Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search. Think of 'Hubs Identify Top Sites' to recall the roles of hubs (pointers) and authorities (info sources).
keyPoints: PageRank is a core algorithm that ranks web pages based on the quantity and quality of links pointing to them, treating the web as a graph.
TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) is a statistical measure used to evaluate how important a word is to a document in a collection.
HITS (Hyperlink-Induced Topic Search) algorithm identifies good 'hubs' (pages that link to many good authorities) and good 'authorities' (pages that are linked to by many good hubs).
commonMistakes: Confusing PageRank with simple keyword matching. PageRank is about link analysis, not the text content on the page itself.
Assuming a higher TF (Term Frequency) always means a more relevant document. A word that appears too frequently across all documents (high DF) will have its importance reduced by the IDF component.
Treating HITS and PageRank as the same. HITS produces two scores (hub and authority) for a page and is query-dependent, while PageRank produces a single, global importance score for each page.
